text.text: ().

### Prerequisites

This text will delve deeper into the multiplication concepts explored in Chapter 4 of the first volume of the *First Steps* section (<htcite0>) e no texto anterior (<htcite1>).

### Introduction

When hiking in regions such as Peabirú, in the Americas (*Abya Yala*) (**Figura 1**), we don't always find flat paths. There are ups and downs along the entire route.

This detail of the path leads us to a reflection:

Figure 1: Representation of the Peabirú path followed by Aleixo Garcia. © Mapswire (). Compartilhado sob a licença Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International (CC BY-NC-SA 4.0).

Reflection 1: Higher up, lower down...

Considering what we saw in the previous text (), what do you think will happen when we multiply positive and negative numbers by the same number greater than 1?

And what if we multiply them by a number between 0 and 1?

### Negative multiplication table or multiplying by -1?

In the previous text, when working with the "multiplication table of -1," we saw that the difference between the formulas of this new table and the table we had seen when we studied the multiplication table of 1 was the sign (**Formulas 1 and 2**) (<htcite0>; <htcite1>):

N = 1 × N

Formula 1: Result of multiplying by one.

-N = -1 × N

Formula 2: Result of multiplying by -1.

We must highlight something in the results on the left-hand side of both formulas. When we multiply any number by 1, that means we are counting that quantity once, and therefore the result is the number itself.

Similarly, when we multiply by -1, that means we are counting the same number once, but in the opposite direction.

#### Going Deeper

From the previous highlights, we can rewrite **Formula 2** as:

-1 × N = -1 × ( 1 × N )

Formula 3: Result of multiplying by 1 multiplied by -1.
**Formula 3** indicates that **Formula 2** was obtained by multiplying both sides of **Formula 1** by -1. This fact leads us to another reflection:

Reflection 2: Do you as well?

If, to obtain the result of the "multiplication table of -1," we simply multiply the results of the multiplication table of 1 by -1, could we do the same with other multiplication tables to obtain negative results?

#### Practicing

Answering only "yes" to the previous reflection might be sufficient, but it is better to visualize the changes. Using the following selection box, see how the multiplication tables of positive and negative numbers are identical, the only difference being the direction, that is, the fact that we multiply by -1 to reverse it.

Select a multiplication table and see the relationship explained:
Multiplication table :

### The multiplication tables chart

Just as we extended the multiplication results by -1 to the left (<htcite0>), in **Graph 1** below we place the results of multiplying values by 1 through 9:
Graph 1: Points of the multiplication tables from 1 to 9 connected, forming a straight line. Graph made with the library.

#### Studying the graph

To better understand what happens to the values as we multiply by a certain value — i.e., we repeatedly add the same amount — let us list some characteristics of the graphs. To better visualize what we are describing, click on the names of the multiplication tables to hide them:
- Regardless of which multiplication table we are working with, from the left side, the values will always increase. Likewise, from the right side, the values will always decrease.
- There is a convergence point among all multiplication tables: the number zero. This occurs because any number multiplied by zero equals zero (<htcite0>);
- The previous conclusion can be expressed in another way: as we move toward the center, the values tend toward zero. Mathematically, we say that, in the limit as the values approach the center from either the left or the right, the result of the multiplication tends to zero (<htcite1>).
- The larger the multiplication table — that is, the number by which we always multiply, also known as the constant — the faster the values will move away from the horizontal line. On the other hand, the smaller the multiplier, the closer we will be to it. Precisely because there is a line moving away and, thus, forming an angle between this new line and the horizontal line, we also call the multiplying constant the slope (<htcite2>).

Reflection 3: There is still this

In the description of the characteristics of the multiplication tables graphs, we have a visual effect where the 1 times table is above all others on the left side, while on the right side, it is below all of them.

Does this match the descriptions?

If the constant by which we multiply were 0.5 — that is, if we made a 0.5 times table — what would that line look like on the graph?

We will answer only the second question of the reflection, in order to help you with the first one.

If we use the value 0.5 and create a multiplication table with it, on the left side it will have values above all others, since it is a value between 0 and 1 (<htcite0>).

From this first result, we can also infer that, on the right side of the graph, a line will be created with values below the line of the 1 times table, indicating that the deviation of this line from the horizontal will be smaller than that of all the others.

### In Life

When we walk on inclined surfaces such as the Peabirú (**Figura 1**), the multiplication table is constantly used in different scenarios, which we will now describe.

#### Machu Picchu

In Machu Picchu, Peru, in the Americas (*Abya Yala*), on the Pacific side, we can see that there are inclinations in the constructions. These inclinations can be recreated with the multiplication tables we have built so far.
Figure 2: Photo of the city of Machu Picchu, Peru. Photo taken on , during a visit to the city.

#### In Nature

Our species, *Homo sapiens* (<htcite0>), learned to build by repeating what we see in nature.

In **Figure 2**, we can observe that Machu Picchu is positioned in a range of mountains that have their own inclinations.

In addition to these larger inclinations, when walking through the city (**Figure 3**), we can observe in more detail elevations that are also present in stones and on paths, as in the Peabirú.
Figure 3: Interior photo of the city of Machu Picchu, Peru. Photo taken on , during a visit to the city.
text.imgdesc: In this photo, we see animals positioned between stones and an inclined region of Machu Picchu.

text.text:
#### Modulating

The constructions we see in Machu Picchu are built in stages, which can also be called modules.

This is not the only new concept presented in the previous text (<htcite0>) and in this one. There is also the process of recreating or using nature through functions, which connect one group of numbers to another by means of a rule (<htcite1>).

For example, we can infer that the height we reach when walking in a mountainous region can be expressed by **Formula 4**:

source.text: Stover, Christopher e Weisstein, Eric W.

text.text:
Height we reach = Slope of the path × number of steps

Formula 4: Formula for the height we reach according to the steps.

exercise_v2.question: Podemos entender as tabuadas negativas são rigorosamente as mesmas tabuadas que normalmente usamos?
exercise_v2.additionalInfo: Uma tabuada negativa, como está sendo definido no History Tracers, são somas consecutivas de um mesmo valor.

exercise_v2.question: Existe relação entre a tabuana que normalmente usamos e a tabuada negativa?
exercise_v2.additionalInfo: Por executarmos o mesmo processo de soma continua em ambas, podemos chegar na tabuada negativa realizando multiplicação por -1 em ambos os lados da tabuada tradicional. Este ato indica que estamos mudando a direção e mantendo o valor.

exercise_v2.question: Quando a tabuada (constante que multiplicamos por diferentes números) é um valor maior que outro, por exemplo 9 é maior que 7, isto quer dizer que os valores negativos de 9 também serão mais negativos que 7?
exercise_v2.additionalInfo: Para responder esta pergunta, devemos comparar valores multiplicados na mesma posição horizontal (7 × 1 com 9 × 1, 7 × 2 com 9 × 2...). Na sequência, multiplicamos os resultados por -1. Conforme poderá ser observado, a tabuada de 9 sempre terá valores mais negativos para as mesmas posições da tabuada de 7 e por este motivo, há a inversão vista no Gráficoo 1.
